title: Lagging Fed Raises Risk for the AI Trade

Today, let's talk about the macro now
with Darius Dale, founder and CEO of
macro research for firm 42 macro. It's
been a long time, Darius, my friend. So,
thank you so much for joining the
program here. Look, all the data we've
got this month has shown that things are
holding up. I mean, even the retail
sales today, claims looking okay as
well. I mean, things are looking
resilient, they're looking stable.
Given what we've got on the macro side
and the corporate reads we've got this
earnings season, what has been your
takeaways to the macro setup right now?
Yeah, thanks again for having me. Always
a real pleasure to be here, Sam. Uh so,
the first thing I'll say is that you
kind of take the 30,000 ft view, we're
still in a risk-on market regime. We
expect markets to be generally bullish
over the next 3 to 12 months. However,
when you look at the next 2 to 3 months,
we're starting to see things from a
macro standpoint that are mispriced
relative to broader asset markets that
could cause some trouble. Uh for
starters, the Fed is lagging well beyond
well behind uh its peer central banks in
terms of the amount of tightening that
is priced in over the next 12 months.
So, we could see a catch-up trade uh in
the coming months as the Fed and and
Kevin Warsh and his colleagues on the
Fed readjust their reaction function to
what is a very clearly very hot uh core
inflation dynamic here in the US
economy. Now, on the flip side of that,
I I as evidenced by today's retail sales
print and some other data that we've
received in recent weeks, the US
consumer continues to be fine, the US
economy continues to be fine. And so,
ultimately, we think markets resolve the
next couple of months of potential
volatility positively, but we do believe
that we could be seeing uh uh some sort
of all in the next in the coming weeks
and months.
So, what do you think would be the
catalyst then for the Fed to remove that
easing bias? Given, of course, this
repricing we've seen now of the markets
now thinking, mhm, perhaps we could be
in for a bit of a rate hike come 2027.
Yeah, yeah. So, the catalyst is already
on the table. It's already It's already
been prepared. If you look at, uh for
example, trim mean CPI, which is what
Kevin Warsh highlighted as
what he wants to pivot the Fed's
reaction function to,
the trim mean CPI is in a strong
positive impulse right now. That means a
3-month annualized of 3.4% is greater
than the 6-month annualized of 3%, which
itself is greater than the
year-over-year rate of change of 2.9%,
which obviously is well above uh the
Fed's 2% uh target. And so, the first
thing that he's going to have to do uh
when he joins the FOMC is stop jawboning
rates down and start to listen to his
colleagues on the FOMC who have become
incrementally hawkish in recent months,
so that they can ultimately try to
jawbone a lot of this rates volatility
and currency volatility out of the
market by committing uh to uh stop you
know, stymieing inflation the build-up
of inflation pressure.
Darius, how do you think the market is
likely to react to that given the clear
distinction we're talking about between
previous energy shocks and now, I'm
thinking back to 2022. Is that you know,
obviously conditions were similar in
some ways, but we had a more sort of
hawkish uh rate hiking bias in this
system, which is not what we have now.
And that is what is perhaps the view as
to why we're seeing this resilience in
the stock market and these record highs.
I mean, how would a market react to a
change in that jawboning?
Yeah, it's a great question. Uh probably
not well. So, there's two things that
will cause some issues there. One, we
have uh the return of crowd of bulls
positioning, not quite where we were in
January, but certainly uh something that
it could cause a more material
acceleration to the downside to the
extent the markets get concerned about
these uh hawkish dynamics. And in
secondarily, uh you have the Fed as well
like I said, it's well beyond well
behind uh its peer central banks with
respect to the amount of tightening that
is priced in. So, to the extent markets
have to price in more tightening on the
short rates curve or a pullback of
reserve management purchases, which are
annualizing at about $600 billion,
then you're going to start to see some
uh have
uh creep in. So, in our opinion, you
know, we're still you know, we're in a
very positive tailwind from the
perspective of the global liquidity
cycle. However, those tailwinds are
starting to erode at the margin at the
same time you're getting a hawkish
repricing uh coming out of the Fed.
Those are not good catalysts for
markets.
What's the risk of the Fed not
moving in lockstep or following on from
other central banks? Because arguably
the view is that the US is in better
shape than the rest of the world. I
mean, particularly when you think about
energy independence or a less
vulnerability, particularly with the
Europeans uh obviously the BOE, the ECB,
the circumstances that they have on
their hands right now. I know the
Aussies have already hiked a number of
times this year in a completely
different situation.
But what is the risk of the Fed not
following suit here? I mean, is this
about the dollar?
Well, the risk of them not following
suit is exactly what happened in 2021
and 2022. The less they do now, the more
they're going to have to do later in
terms of catching up and ultimately
rejecting uh this hawkish build-up of
hawkish inflationary uh pressures. Uh
and and the markets are already well
aware of those dynamics you talked about
with respect to the US's relative
resiliency uh with regards to these
inflation pressures and with regards to
the global supply chain pressures.
That's why tech is up 24% uh since our
model signaled that we returned to a
risk-on market regime globally on April
11th. 24% compared to that I think the
next closest sector is up I think about
less than 5%. And so, clearly the
markets and global investors have
crowded capital aggressively crowded
capital into our mega-cap growth sector,
particularly technology, anything
exposed to AI. However, the US is not
resilient to the global liquidity cycle.
The US is the world's largest net
international investment deficit
economy. If the supply chains remain
broken and the Federal Reserve is
pivoting its reaction function in a
hawkish direction, both of those things
are going to cause a drawdown in global
liquidity that would ultimately cause a
drawdown or wipeout in all that crowded
bull's positioning in AI trade.
Okay. And obviously the AI trade has
held up pretty well in the face of these
higher yields. I mean, obviously with a
10-year at 444 and then a 30-year at
what? North of 5% but you just wonder
how much the bond vigilantes will push
the Fed into a position where, you know,
it might have to remove that easing bias
as well, particularly if you look at the
2-year. Darius, we've completely run out
of time but fascinating chat. Thanks so
much for jumping in and joining us this
morning. Darius Dale, there founder and
CEO of macro research firm 42 macro.
